Name: Malabar Flameback
How_to_identify: Endemic to the Western Ghats. Distinguished from Greater Flameback by its smaller size and shorter bill. Features a red rump and a split black malar stripe. Male has a full red crest; female has a black crest with white spots.
Diet: Ants, beetles, and larvae found in tree bark

Name: White-naped Woodpecker
Habitat: Open forest, scrub, and wooded savannas
How_to_identify: Large woodpecker with a white hind-neck extending down to a V-shape on the back. Wings are golden-yellow. Male has a red crown; female has a yellow crown. Unique among Indian flamebacks for the yellow crown in females.

Name: Pale-headed Woodpecker
Habitat: Bamboo forests and broadleaf evergreen forests
How_to_identify: Diagnostic pale-cream or yellowish head contrasting with a dull olive-green and brownish-red body. Short, pale bill. Male has a small crimson patch on the crown which the female lacks.
Diet: Ants and beetle larvae, particularly those in bamboo

Name: Bay Woodpecker
Habitat: Subtropical moist montane forests and dense ravines
Best_seen_at: Eaglenest Wildlife Sanctuary (Arunachal Pradesh), Neora Valley (West Bengal)
How_to_identify: Medium-sized, dark rufous-brown woodpecker with heavy black barring on the wings and tail. Features a pale-yellow bill. Male has a bright crimson patch on the sides of the neck/nape; female lacks this red patch.

Name: Rufous Woodpecker
Habitat: Open forests, scrub, and deciduous woodlands
How_to_identify: Entirely rufous-brown bird with fine black barring. Short, black bill and a slightly rounded tail. Male has a small crimson patch below the eye; female lacks this red markings. Often associated with 'Crematogaster' ant nests.
Diet: Ants and termites exclusively; known to nest inside active ant nests

Name: Great Slaty Woodpecker
Habitat: Old-growth broadleaf forests and sal forests
Best_seen_at: Dudhwa National Park (Uttar Pradesh), Jim Corbett National Park (Uttarakhand)
How_to_identify: The largest woodpecker in India. Uniform slate-grey plumage with a long neck and very long bill. Throat is yellowish. Male has a small red malar patch which the female lacks.
Diet: Large social insects like ants, termites, and honeybee larvae

Name: Collared Falconet
Best_seen_at: Jim Corbett National Park (Uttarakhand), Manas National Park (Assam)
How_to_identify: Tiny raptor with a distinctive white collar and white forehead/eyebrow. Underparts are rufous on the thighs and vent. Distinguished from Pied Falconet by the white collar and rufous on the belly.
Diet: Large insects (dragonflies, butterflies) and occasionally small birds or lizards

Name: Pied Falconet
Habitat: Deciduous forest edges and wooded foothills
How_to_identify: Tiny raptor with stark black-and-white plumage. All-white underparts and black upperparts with a white forehead/eyebrow. Lacks the rufous belly and white collar of the Collared Falconet.
Diet: Primarily insects, also small birds and mammals

Name: Lesser Kestrel
Best_seen_at: Rollapadu Wildlife Sanctuary (Andhra Pradesh) during passage migration
How_to_identify: Small falcon. Male has unspotted rufous back and blue-grey wing-coverts (distinguishes it from Common Kestrel). Female is very similar to Common Kestrel but has pale claws (not black) and is generally more gregarious.
Diet: Mainly insects (orthopterans), but also small lizards and rodents

Name: Common Kestrel
Habitat: Open country, fields, and mountain slopes
Best_seen_at: Tal Chhapar Sanctuary (Rajasthan), widespread in winter
How_to_identify: Long-tailed falcon known for hovering. Male has a grey head and tail with a black terminal band, and a spotted rufous back. Female is rufous-brown with heavy barring and black claws.
Diet: Small mammals (voles, mice), lizards, and large insects
